steps.title: Spot the contrast signaled by however
steps.content:
The passage presents two opposing views: political scientists say ads should reduce knowledge gaps (reduce the differences in political knowledge), while cognitive scientists note that prior knowledge helps learning (existing domain knowledge facilitates acquisition).

The word "however" marks this direct contrast.

steps.title: Apply the graph evidence
steps.content:
The graph confirms the cognitive scientists' prediction: among high-knowledge participants, viewing the ad raised quiz scores from roughly 30% to roughly 54%, while low-knowledge participants stayed flat at roughly 30%. That widened the knowledge gap rather than narrowing it -- the opposite of what political scientists predicted.

This supports the cognitive scientists' view that prior knowledge facilitates acquisition of new information.

steps.title: Apply the contrast to the answer choices
steps.content:
Choice B states that the ads produced an effect opposite to what political scientists expect, which is exactly what the graph shows -- the gap widened, not narrowed.

  • A: eliminated -- ads did not benefit both groups equally; low-knowledge participants showed no score gain.
  • C: eliminated -- the cognitive phenomenon did occur; high-knowledge participants learned more from the ad.
  • D: eliminated -- the gap was not narrowed; it was widened.

That matches choice B -- B is correct.
